title: Catechist Guide — Holy Orders
html:
Catechist Guide

To prepare and lead the lesson. For catechist use only.

Lesson Summary

Estimated duration: 50-60 minutes (with activities, 90 minutes)

Central theme: Holy Orders is the sacrament through which Christ, the Supreme and Eternal High Priest, prolongs his ministry through men configured with him in three degrees: bishop, priest, and deacon.

Objectives:

  • That the student understands the three degrees of Holy Orders and the difference between the common and ministerial priesthood.
  • That they discover the biblical roots of the priesthood, from Melchizedek to the Last Supper.
  • That they appreciate the profound meaning of celibacy and the mission of the priest.
  • That they awaken or renew their appreciation for the priests in their lives and pray for vocations.

Key Scripture verse: John 15:16 («You did not choose me, but I chose you»)

Catechism references: CCC 1533-1600

Suggested Lesson Structure

Section Duration What the Catechist Does
1. Welcome and icebreaker 5 min Welcome, create atmosphere. Ask: «Do you know any priests? What image comes to mind when you think of a priest?» Listen without judging.
2. The Story of the Curé of Ars 8 min Tell the story of St. John Vianney in your own words, highlighting his human weakness and God's power acting through him. Ask: «What surprises you about this story?»
3. Main Content 25 min Present the sections: three degrees (use the table), OT prefigurations, Christ the High Priest, institution at the Last Supper, in persona Christi, celibacy, triple mission. Read together CCC 1546-1547. Emphasize that the priest is instrument, not source.
4. Group Activity 10 min Choose workbook activity. Children: draw the three degrees with symbols. Youth: letter to a priest or Bible search for the laying on of hands. Adults: reflect on their baptismal priesthood.
5. Sharing Together 8 min Voluntary sharing of what they discovered. Steer the dialogue toward gratitude for priests and prayer for vocations.
6. Closing Prayer 4 min Pray together the workbook prayer or the Tu es Sacerdos in aeternum. Recall John 15:16.

Notes for the Catechist

About Saint John Mary Vianney

The Holy Curé of Ars (1786-1859) is the patron saint of parish priests. His story is especially useful for this lesson because it demolishes the idea that a priest must be a superhero. Vianney was a stutterer, nearly illiterate when he entered the seminary, systematically failed Latin, and was ordained only because the vicar general said: «This young man is devout; if he does not know much, at least he knows how to pray.» Yet his confessional transformed France. The contrast between his natural weakness and the supernatural power of his ministry is the best catechesis on in persona Christi: God does not need our talents; he needs our availability.

Saint John XXIII wrote about him in the encyclical Sacerdotii Nostri Primordia (1959), and Benedict XVI proclaimed a Year for Priests (2009-2010) on the 150th anniversary of his death. Recommend the biography by Francis Trochu to your students.

About the Common and Ministerial Priesthood

This point is doctrinally crucial and often misunderstood. Some Catholics think the priest is «holier» or «superior»; others, in reaction, think he is unnecessary because «we are all priests.» The catechist must maintain the Vatican II balance: both priesthoods share in the one priesthood of Christ but differ essentially, not only in degree (Lumen Gentium 10). A useful image: the layperson sanctifies the world from within, like leaven in the dough; the ministerial priest nourishes the laity with the sacraments so they can be leaven. Without priests there is no Eucharist; without the Eucharist the Church dies.

About Celibacy

Celibacy is one of the most controversial topics. Prepare for questions and objections. Some keys for responding:

  • It is not dogma but a discipline the Church can change (Eastern Catholic Churches ordain married men). Do not enter a dialectical battle over this.
  • What matters is the meaning: celibacy is a sign of total love and an anticipation of heaven. It is not «not marrying»; it is «marrying everyone» with Christ's love.
  • Quote Saint Josemaría: celibacy does not extinguish love; it universalizes it.
  • If someone brings up abuse scandals, receive the pain with honesty. Celibacy rightly lived does not cause abuse; sin and infidelity do. Pray for the purification of the Church and for the victims.

About the Ordination of Women

This question will likely arise. The Church's position (CCC 1577-1578, John Paul II's Ordinatio Sacerdotalis) is that the Church has no authority to ordain women because Christ chose only men as apostles, and this fact is sacramental, not cultural. It is not about capability (Mary is holier than all the apostles combined) but about fidelity to the sacramental sign as Christ instituted it. Answer with clarity but without arrogance. If the group is mature, you may add that the Church values women immensely — without lay and religious women, the Church would not exist — but vocations are different, not superior or inferior.

Adaptation by Age Group

Children (7-12): Center on the Curé of Ars story and the three degrees with drawings. Use the paintbrush metaphor: God is the painter, the priest is the brush. Ask: «What things does a priest do for us?» Drawing the symbols of bishop, priest, and deacon works very well.

Youth (13-17): The topic of celibacy and personal vocation touches them closely. Ask directly: «And if God called you, would you have considered it?» Quote Carlo Acutis: he had everything a young person desires and chose God. The letter to a priest is an activity that often moves them. Showing YouTube clips by Fr. Mike Schmitz or Ascension Presents also works well.

New adults: They may have prejudices about the priesthood: negative experiences, incomprehension of celibacy, distrust of the hierarchy. Do not become defensive. Listen first, then explain. The testimony of converts like Scott Hahn (a former Protestant pastor who discovered the theology of the priesthood) can be very helpful.

Formed adults: Deepen sacramental theology: the ontological (not merely functional) difference of the priesthood, the indelible character, the relationship between the sacrifice of the Mass and the sacrifice of Calvary, the question of the validity of Anglican orders (Apostolicae Curae, Leo XIII). Do not shy away from these topics if there is interest.

Possible Difficult Questions (and How to Answer Them)

«Why do I need a priest if I can pray directly to God?»

It is true that you can and should pray directly to God. But the sacraments are not prayers; they are actions of Christ himself. Baptism, Confession, and the Eucharist require an ordained minister because Christ willed to give us these gifts through men who act in his name. It is not that the priest is a middleman who separates us from God; he is an instrument God uses to reach us in a tangible and certain way. Jesus could have left a book; he left a Church with shepherds.

«Isn't celibacy an unnatural imposition?»

Freely accepted celibacy is not an imposition: it is a vocation. No one is forced to be a priest. Those who choose celibacy do so out of love, not repression. Just as a father gives up free time for his children — and no one calls that unnatural — the priest renounces a family of his own to serve the great family of God. Human nature can be lived in many ways; celibacy is one of them and has produced some of the most fruitful human beings ever: Francis of Assisi, Mother Teresa, Don Bosco.

«Why can't women be priests?»

It is not about capacity, dignity, or talent: the Blessed Virgin Mary is superior to all priests combined. It is about fidelity to the sacramental sign. Christ, who broke with many customs of his time (he spoke with women in public, received them as disciples), nonetheless chose only men for the Twelve. The Church feels bound by that choice. But remember that most canonized saints are women, that the Church rests on the quiet work of religious sisters, catechists, and mothers of families, and that each person's vocation is different and valuable.

«I have known priests who were bad people. How can I believe in the priesthood?»

You are completely right to feel pain or disappointment. Bad priests cause terrible harm, and they will answer to God. But the gift of the priesthood does not depend on the holiness of the minister. Judas was an apostle and Christ knew it. The Church is made up of sinners; if we wait for all priests to be perfect saints before believing in the priesthood, we will never believe. Look to Christ, who acts through fragile instruments, and pray for the conversion of those who fail.

«How do I know if God is calling me to the priesthood?»

Vocations are discerned, not divined. Three clues: (1) Do you feel an attraction to ministry, even if it scares you? (2) Have people you trust suggested it to you? (3) When you pray about it, do you feel peace? If so, speak with a priest or the vocations director of your diocese. Entering the seminary is not signing a lifelong contract; it is beginning a path of serious discernment. God does not get angry if you try and it is not your path.

Additional Resources

  • YouCat 248-259: Accessible explanation of Holy Orders in youth language.
  • Francis Trochu: The Curé of Ars. Classic biography, highly readable, full of anecdotes.
  • Saint Josemaría: Homily «A Priest Forever» in Loving the Church. Free online.
  • Ascension Presents: Fr. Mike Schmitz, videos on the priesthood.
  • Real + True (OSV): Episode on Holy Orders at osvnews.com.
  • Scott Hahn: Rome Sweet Home, testimony of a Protestant pastor who discovered the Catholic priesthood.
  • Hakuna Group Music: «Tuyo soy» («I Am Yours»), a vocational hymn.
  • Gregorian chant: Tu es Sacerdos in aeternum (Ps 110:4), sung at ordinations.

Catechist's Prayer

Lord Jesus, Supreme and Eternal High Priest,
who at the Last Supper gave your apostles
the power to perpetuate your sacrifice of love,
I pray for the men you have called
and will call to ordained ministry.
Sustain the bishops in their mission as shepherds,
the priests in their faithful service,
the deacons in their witness of charity.
And grant me, catechist, the grace
to speak of this sacrament with reverence, truth, and love,
so that my words may kindle in others
gratitude and generosity.
Amen.

Print Catechist Guide
